questions.stem: A 24-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks gestation presents with a two-day history of frontal headache and visual disturbances. Her blood pressure is 155/98 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 2+ protein. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: New-onset hypertension with proteinuria after 20 weeks, along with headache and visual disturbances, is classic for pre-eclampsia. Gestational hypertension lacks proteinuria; chronic hypertension predates pregnancy; HELLP would have additional lab abnormalities; eclampsia requires seizures.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman at 31 weeks gestation complains of epigastric pain, nausea, and vomiting for 24 hours. She has no headache or visual changes. Blood pressure is 158/96 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 2+ protein. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Epigastric pain with hypertension and proteinuria is a classic presentation of pre-eclampsia with hepatic involvement, even without typical headache/visual symptoms. Acute fatty liver of pregnancy would have hypoglycemia and coagulopathy; HELLP requires thrombocytopenia and hemolysis; viral gastroenteritis would not cause hypertension/proteinuria; cholecystitis would have right upper quadrant pain and tenderness.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks gestation is noted to have blood pressure of 170/110 mmHg. She has a severe headache and blurred vision. Urine dipstick shows 3+ protein. Her platelet count is 90 x10^9/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hypertension (170/110) with proteinuria and cerebral symptoms meets criteria for severe pre-eclampsia. Although thrombocytopenia is present, it can occur in severe pre-eclampsia without full HELLP criteria (which requires hemolysis and elevated liver enzymes). HELLP syndrome typically includes evidence of hemolysis and elevated liver enzymes. Eclampsia involves seizures. ITP would not cause hypertension/proteinuria.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman with known pre-eclampsia at 34 weeks gestation suddenly develops a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Her blood pressure was 180/110 mmHg one hour prior. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A seizure in a woman with pre-eclampsia is diagnostic of eclampsia. Hypertensive encephalopathy may cause headache and altered mental status but typically not seizures; epileptic seizure would be unpredictable and doesn't require pre-eclampsia; intracranial haemorrhage may occur but is a complication; pseudoseizure is less likely.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with chronic hypertension (on labetalol 200 mg BD) at 28 weeks gestation in her second pregnancy. Her blood pressure is 150/92 mmHg. She has new-onset 1+ proteinuria on dipstick. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In a woman with pre-existing hypertension, new-onset proteinuria after 20 weeks suggests superimposed pre-eclampsia. Gestational hypertension would be new hypertension without proteinuria, but she already has hypertension. Chronic hypertension would not have new proteinuria. Renal disease is possible but less likely with proteinuria developing after 20 weeks.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks gestation attends triage with a blood pressure of 148/92 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 2+ protein. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm significant proteinuria?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends urine PCR as the first-line test for quantifying proteinuria in suspected pre-eclampsia due to its speed and reliability. A 24-hour collection is an alternative but less practical. Dipstick is only a screening test.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman at 36 weeks gestation presents with blood pressure 170/110 mmHg, frontal headache, and epigastric pain. She has no visual disturbance. Which laboratory test is most specific for assessing liver involvement in severe pre-eclampsia?
questions.explanation: In pre-eclampsia, liver involvement is typically hepatocellular, and NICE recommends monitoring ALT (or AST) as it is more specific for hepatocyte damage than LDH (which also rises in haemolysis) or cholestatic markers like GGT and bilirubin.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman presents 5 days after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery with a blood pressure of 160/100 mmHg and severe frontal headache. Urine dipstick is negative for protein. Which investigation is most appropriate to help confirm a diagnosis of postpartum pre-eclampsia?
questions.explanation: Postpartum pre-eclampsia can present with proteinuria that may not be detected on dipstick; urine PCR is more sensitive and recommended by NICE. CT head is indicated if neurological symptoms suggest a differential like cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, but proteinuria assessment is key for pre-eclampsia diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation with suspected pre-eclampsia (BP 150/95 mmHg, mild headache) undergoes a placental growth factor (PlGF) test. The result is low (less than 100 pg/mL). According to NICE guidance, what does this result indicate?
options: High risk of needing delivery within the next 14 days
Low risk of developing pre-eclampsia in the next 7 days
questions.explanation: NICE recommends PlGF testing to rule out the need for delivery within 14 days; a low PlGF (less than 100 pg/mL) is associated with a high likelihood of needing delivery within 14 days due to worsening pre-eclampsia. A normal PlGF can help avoid unnecessary intervention.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks gestation is diagnosed with mild pre-eclampsia (BP 150/95 mmHg, urine protein 2+). She has no symptoms. Which is the most appropriate first-line oral antihypertensive?
questions.explanation: NICE CG133 recommends labetalol as first-line oral antihypertensive for non-severe hypertension in pregnancy. Nifedipine MR is an alternative if labetalol is not tolerated. Methyldopa is now second-line, hydralazine is used intravenously for severe hypertension, and amlodipine is not preferred in pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with severe pre-eclampsia at 32 weeks gestation develops a generalised tonic-clonic seizure. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for seizure prophylaxis after the acute episode?
questions.explanation: IV magnesium sulfate is the drug of choice for prevention and treatment of eclampsia (NICE CG133). Diazepam and lorazepam are second-line for status epilepticus but not first-line for eclampsia. Phenytoin and levetiracetam are not recommended.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation with HELLP syndrome (platelets 45 × 10^9/L) complains of severe headache. Which analgesic is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac) inhibit platelet aggregation and are contraindicated in HELLP syndrome with severe thrombocytopenia due to increased bleeding risk. Paracetamol, codeine, morphine, and tramadol are safe alternatives.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with asthma and chronic hypertension (on methyldopa) develops pre-eclampsia at 30 weeks gestation (BP 155/105 mmHg, proteinuria). She is asymptomatic. Which antihypertensive is contraindicated in this patient?
questions.explanation: Labetalol is a non-selective beta-blocker and can cause bronchospasm, thus contraindicated in asthma. Nifedipine, methyldopa, hydralazine, and prazosin are safer alternatives.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a history of pre-eclampsia in her previous pregnancy is now 10 weeks pregnant. She has a BMI of 32. Which intervention is recommended to reduce her risk of developing pre-eclampsia?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends low-dose aspirin (75 mg) daily from 12 weeks gestation for women at high risk of pre-eclampsia. High-dose aspirin is not typically used for this purpose. Calcium supplementation may benefit those with low calcium intake, but aspirin is the first-line prevention. Folic acid is for neural tube defect prevention; labetalol is a treatment, not prevention.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks' gestation is seen in the antenatal clinic. Her blood pressure is 150/95 mmHg, and urine dipstick shows 1+ protein. She is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange induction of labour at 37 weeks
Repeat blood pressure measurement after 30 minutes' rest
questions.explanation: In a first presentation of possible pre-eclampsia, hypertension must first be confirmed with a second reading after rest before proceeding with further investigations or treatment. NICE recommends confirming BP ≥140/90 on two occasions at least 4 hours apart, or a single reading ≥160/110.

questions.stem: A 36-week pregnant woman with known pre-eclampsia has a blood pressure of 145/95 mmHg and proteinuria 2+. She has no headache, epigastric pain, or visual disturbances. Her last blood pressure was 140/90. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Repeat blood pressure measurement in 4 hours
questions.explanation: In pre-eclampsia, antihypertensive treatment should be initiated when BP is persistently ≥140/90. Oral labetalol is first-line. Admission and monitoring are important, but the immediate action is to treat hypertension to reduce maternal risk.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks' gestation presents to the emergency department with a blood pressure of 170/110 mmHg, proteinuria 3+, severe headache, and epigastric pain. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Repeat blood pressure measurement in 30 minutes
Give intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g bolus
questions.explanation: Severe hypertension (≥160/110) requires urgent antihypertensive therapy, typically IV labetalol or oral nifedipine. While magnesium sulfate is indicated for eclampsia prevention, blood pressure control takes precedence to reduce risk of maternal stroke.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman, day 1 postpartum following a vaginal delivery for pre-eclampsia, develops a severe headache and has a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure. Her blood pressure is 160/100 mmHg despite oral labetalol. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Transfer to the intensive care unit
Give intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g bolus
questions.explanation: This is eclampsia; the priority is to stop the seizure. Magnesium sulfate is the first-line anticonvulsant. Blood pressure control and investigations (e.g., CT head) follow after seizure termination.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with chronic hypertension presents at 34 weeks' gestation. Her blood pressure is 150/95 mmHg, and a urine dipstick shows trace protein. She has no symptoms. Her booking BP was 140/90. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange induction of labour at 37 weeks
questions.explanation: New-onset proteinuria in a woman with chronic hypertension suggests superimposed pre-eclampsia. NICE recommends using sFlt-1/PlGF ratio to rule in or rule out pre-eclampsia before 35 weeks, as it provides rapid diagnostic information. Starting antihypertensives may be needed but not immediately if BP is borderline; admission can be guided by results.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks gestation presents for routine antenatal check. She feels well. Blood pressure is 152/98 mmHg, and urinalysis shows 1+ protein. Which single finding would most clearly mandate admission and escalation of care?
questions.explanation: Headache and visual disturbance are symptoms of severe pre-eclampsia (cerebral involvement). NICE defines severe pre-eclampsia as BP ≥160/110 or symptoms. This patient has mild hypertension and 1+ protein, but symptoms escalate to severe disease requiring admission. The other options are not threshold-level abnormalities alone.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation with known pre-eclampsia has been managed as an outpatient. On review, her blood pressure is 165/110 mmHg, and she is asymptomatic. Which single parameter most clearly indicates that she now meets criteria for severe pre-eclampsia and should be admitted?
questions.explanation: NICE defines severe pre-eclampsia as sustained BP ≥160/110 mmHg, even without symptoms. This alone mandates admission. The other options are abnormalities often seen but not the primary threshold for severe disease. Proteinuria quantification (≥2+) is not a severity criterion in NICE.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman at 39 weeks gestation is admitted after a generalised tonic-clonic seizure. Her blood pressure is 170/110 mmHg, and urinalysis shows 3+ protein. She is now post-ictal. Which single diagnosis is most likely to explain the seizure?
questions.explanation: In a pregnant woman with hypertension and proteinuria, a seizure is eclampsia until proven otherwise. This is a life-threatening complication of pre-eclampsia. Other causes are possible but far less likely given the typical presentation.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation with pre-eclampsia develops epigastric pain and nausea. Bloods show: Hb 100 g/L, platelets 80 × 10^9/L, AST 150 IU/L, ALT 120 IU/L, bilirubin 40 µmol/L. Which single finding is most specific for HELLP syndrome?
questions.explanation: HELLP syndrome requires haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets. Haemolysis (e.g., elevated bilirubin, low haptoglobin) is the defining component that distinguishes it from severe pre-eclampsia alone. Thrombocytopenia and transaminitis are seen in both. Pain and hypertension are non-specific.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with chronic hypertension on labetalol presents at 28 weeks gestation with BP 150/95 mmHg and new-onset 1+ proteinuria. She has no headache or epigastric pain. Which single finding would most strongly suggest that she has developed superimposed pre-eclampsia rather than gestational hypertension?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends PlGF testing to diagnose pre-eclampsia in the context of chronic hypertension. A low PlGF (<100 pg/mL) is highly suggestive of superimposed pre-eclampsia. The other options are non-specific: proteinuria may be due to other causes, urate can be raised in normal pregnancy, and weight gain is common.
